bull_thesis:
Puig is a rare combination of a genuinely compounding, founder-run premium-beauty platform and a live, hard-dated acquisition catalyst, trading at a deep-value multiple. The operating engine is corroborated by numbers, not by story alone: revenue grew from 2.59 billion euros to 5.04 billion euros (2021 to 2025), a three-year compound annual growth rate of 11.7%, with the company outperforming a decelerating premium-beauty market for eight straight quarters as a public company. EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortisation) margin expanded to 20.7% in fiscal year 2025 from 20.2%, against a gross margin above 75% that ranks among the highest in the industry. Free cash flow inflected sharply: trailing twelve months free cash flow of 1.28 billion euros, up 38% year on year, with a three-year compound annual growth rate of 41.4% and 64% cash conversion. The balance sheet is pristine: net-debt-to-EBITDA leverage fell to 0.7 times in fiscal year 2025, essentially negligible, alongside a return on equity of 28.6%.

Guidance credibility is high. Management guided fiscal year 2025 to the lower end of their 6-8% like-for-like growth target mid-year, raised the call to the middle of the range in October, and delivered 7.8% at the top end — a consistent under-promise, over-deliver pattern that matches the 67% earnings-per-share beat rate. The makeup category's weakness (down 1.3% in fiscal year 2024), explained at the time as a timing issue rather than a demand problem, was vindicated when it rebounded to 13.7% like-for-like growth in fiscal year 2025. Charlotte Tilbury remains distributed in only about one-fifth of its potential footprint, with a locked partnership to 2031 and a path to full ownership, a self-funded second growth engine with Boots UK, Amazon, and Latin America rollout still ahead.

The catalyst is the event that could force the market to reprice the stock: on 23 March 2026, Puig confirmed ongoing discussions about a potential business combination with Estee Lauder (reaffirmed by the incoming chief executive on 1 May 2026), executed alongside a deliberate chief-executive succession — the deputy chief executive appointed mid-2025, replaced by Jose Manuel Muniesa in the first quarter of 2026 — and a Capital Markets Day postponed specifically because of the deal. A founder chief executive stepping back from the earnings stage and shelving standalone guidance immediately before a combination with a US strategic is a textbook signal that a corporate control event is substantively in motion, not merely exploratory. This is a structural special situation in the merger-and-acquisition lane, landing while the US and EU regulatory environment is open and permissive. The deep-value screen anchors an intrinsic fair value at roughly 64 euros (the Buffett-style Graham calculation) against a price of about 16 euros — a 332% indicated margin of safety (enterprise value divided by revenue, compared with intrinsic value). Even after a heavy discount for the dual-class share-count ambiguity described below, a high-quality, free-cash-flow-generating, deleveraged compounder with a live combination catalyst is structurally cheap.

Conditions that must hold for the thesis to pay off: first, the Estee Lauder combination reaches definitive terms at or above standalone intrinsic value — whether through a cash premium or an accretive exchange ratio — or alternatively the talks fail cleanly and the standalone compounder re-rates on its own merits through APAC (Asia-Pacific) acceleration and Charlotte Tilbury distribution whitespace; second, fragrance-category moderation remains a deceleration, not an outright contraction; third, the share count underlying the 64 euro fair value is reconciled to confirm the gap is real and not an artifact of the dual-class structure.
bear_thesis:
The 332% margin-of-safety headline is the trap, not the opportunity. It rests on a 64 euro intrinsic value against a roughly 16 euro price, but the input data carries an unreconciled dual-class contradiction: fiscal year 2025 adjusted earnings per share of 1.04 euros computed on the full share base of roughly 1.1 billion shares, versus a trailing-twelve-months diluted earnings per share of 1.99 euros computed on the free-float denominator only. The same structural issue distorts the Altman Z-score (a statistical measure of financial distress) to 2.0, a figure that looks alarming but is almost certainly a measurement artifact of the dual-class market capitalisation input rather than genuine financial stress. Resolve the denominator and a large portion of the apparent upside evaporates into a data-plumbing error: on the company's own 1.04 euros earnings per share the stock is not a 75% discount to fair value, and the discounted-cash-flow fair value in the screener is 16.2 euros — essentially the current price. The individual valuation methods are wildly dispersed: discounted cash flow +1%, earnings power value (the no-growth earnings-power value, computed without assuming any future reinvestment) shows a margin of safety of -100%, Graham +63%. At -100%, earnings power value says the business does not generate enough earnings at current scale to justify the price even before paying for future growth.

The operating story is also quietly downshifting beneath the deal noise. The Fragrance and Fashion segment — 73% of revenue — decelerated from 11-14% like-for-like growth in 2024 to 3.9% in the first quarter of 2026. Group like-for-like growth fell to 4.7%, the slowest print in the dataset. Management cut its own fragrance-category forecast from 6-7% growth to low single digits. Reported value market share slipped from 11.5% to 11.1%, half-conceded as a genuine loss to promotional competitors in Latin America. Gross margin is flagging as contracting, down 0.35 standard deviations from its historical peak, moving from 75.8% in the first half of 2025 to 75.1% for the full fiscal year 2025.

Headline growth has repeatedly been flattered by a sell-in versus sell-out timing effect (the difference between shipments to retailers and actual end-consumer purchases): makeup's 18.8% growth in the third quarter of 2025 was roughly half a one-time Amazon US pipeline fill — management conceded 'Amazon represents about half of that 18.8% growth'. APAC's 35.8% growth was partly a subsidiary-consolidation uplift management said they did not expect to repeat in 2026. An explicit Amazon-anniversary normalisation air-pocket is expected in the second quarter of 2026. Strip the pipeline fills and the underlying demand is decelerating, mid-single-digit, FX-battered (negative 4% reported drag in the first quarter of 2026), with a fresh Middle East and travel-retail headwind (negative 1.2% group impact; March travel-retail reportedly down roughly 50%).

The catalyst is binary and not controlled by Puig. The Estee Lauder talks describe 'a potential business combination where the two companies will potentially merge their business' — language consistent with a stock-for-stock merger, not a clean cash acquisition. Even a successful outcome may re-rate Puig only to a combined-entity multiple plus contested synergy value rather than delivering a hard cash premium. A failed deal removes the only near-term re-rating force, dropping the stock back onto decelerating standalone fundamentals at a full discounted-cash-flow multiple. There is no share buyback, asset sale, or dividend step-up that Puig itself controls to force price convergence. The insider buy ratio is 0.00 and smart-money positioning scores 26 out of 100 — neither the family nor institutions are buying the gap through open-market purchases. Layer on maximum governance flux — new chief executive, new chief financial officer, a shelved multi-year vision plan, a postponed Capital Markets Day — at exactly the moment the company needs steady leadership, and the bear case becomes clear: a fairly valued (on reconciled share count) decelerating compounder whose entire excess-return case hinges on one binary, terms-opaque merger that may dilute rather than reward minority shareholders.

What confirms the bear: definitive Estee Lauder terms that are a stock-merger at a thin or zero premium, or a deal break that exposes second-quarter 2026 fragrance, FX, and Middle East softness with no catalyst remaining to absorb it.
consensus_delta:
There is no fat 'hidden compounder the market hates' disagreement-with-consensus available here. This is a well-covered, recently listed European large-cap — Santander, CaixaBank, Barclays, Goldman Sachs, Bank of America, Alantra, and JB Capital were all active in the question-and-answer sessions — and the consensus already knows the free cash flow inflection (up 38% year on year) and the eight-quarter market-outperformance streak.

The real place where we disagree with the market is narrower and event-driven, and it cuts both ways. First, the sell-side is genuinely offside on the quality of recent growth: analysts repeatedly accepted headline makeup and APAC prints that management itself conceded were roughly half mechanical — Amazon sell-in 'about half of that 18.8% growth' (30 October 2025), APAC subsidiary-consolidation 'we do not expect to repeat in 2026'. To the extent buy-side models extrapolate those figures, they overstate underlying demand. That is a negative disagreement as the second quarter of 2026 normalises. Second, the screener's 332% margin of safety to a 64 euro fair value is almost certainly not what informed holders believe: the dual-class earnings-per-share contradiction (1.04 euros adjusted versus 1.99 euros trailing-twelve-months diluted) and a discounted-cash-flow fair value of 16.2 euros — roughly the current price — mean the screener's Graham anchor likely overstates the gap. The sell-side is probably correctly valuing the standalone business nearer the discounted-cash-flow estimate than the Graham number, so '332%' is a screener measurement artifact, not a genuine street error to arbitrage.

The one place the market may still be looking the wrong way is in treating the Estee Lauder combination as low-probability or value-neutral. A confirmed, ongoing, board-level potential business combination — with a chief-executive succession choreographed around it and a Capital Markets Day postponed because of it — is more advanced than 'exploratory'. The standalone APAC and Charlotte Tilbury self-help optionality ('distributed only in about one-fifth of the footprint', 'now is the right moment to accelerate') provides a floor that deal-sceptics may underweight. In net terms, this is not a mispriced compounder; it is a fairly-priced compounder wrapped around a binary, terms-opaque merger — the potential payoff is in the event itself, not in a fundamental perception gap.
forcing_function:
The catalyst that forces the re-rating is unusually well-defined and time-sensitive, which is precisely what the current investment environment rewards. The primary catalyst is the Estee Lauder potential business combination, confirmed on 23 March 2026 and reaffirmed as ongoing on 1 May 2026, with the announcement of definitive terms — whether a deal, a ratio and premium, or a clean break — as the binary re-rating trigger. This is a structural special situation in the merger-and-acquisition lane, landing while the US and EU regulatory environment is open and permissive: the Federal Trade Commission and Department of Justice are in a remedy-friendly mode, and the UK Competition and Markets Authority is operating under its 'four pillars' framework. That materially raises the probability that a large cosmetics combination could clear regulatory review.

A critical timing caveat caps conviction. The permissive regulatory window may narrow after the November 2026 US midterm elections — an explicit tripwire. For the forcing function to deliver its full payoff, terms need to crystallise in the first half or summer of 2026; slippage past the midterms raises the risk of a break or increased regulatory scrutiny.

If the deal fails cleanly, three secondary forcing functions exist on the standalone path: first, the postponed Capital Markets Day and new multi-year vision plan, shelved because of the ongoing discussions (1 May 2026), which — if rescheduled — would deliver a fresh standalone strategic re-rating event; second, second-quarter and first-half 2026 results (expected the week of 27 July 2026), the next hard dated print; third, the incoming chief executive's stated APAC-acceleration agenda ('now is the right moment to accelerate', from 11% of group sales into a region representing roughly one-third of the global beauty market).

This thesis does not depend on a central bank interest-rate cut — Puig carries only 0.7 times leverage and is essentially indifferent to financing costs, which is a positive. It is not a hard regulatory-approval event with a single binary outcome date. However, it is a single-driver thesis: everything hinges on the Estee Lauder outcome. That concentration is worth sizing carefully for, even though the driver is company-specific and not correlated to oil prices or technology-capital-expenditure cycles — a genuine diversification benefit for a broader portfolio.
valley_of_death:
Is there a forced liquidation, debt maturity wall, or mechanical selling event that kills the thesis before value can converge? On the balance sheet, no: leverage is 0.7 times net-debt-to-EBITDA in fiscal year 2025, there is no meaningful debt maturity approaching, and free cash flow is running at 1.28 billion euros with 64% cash conversion. Puig is the opposite of a financially stressed company.

The near-term danger is operational and deal-timing, not solvency. Over the next three to nine months there are three concrete hazards. First, second-quarter and first-half 2026 results (expected the week of 27 July 2026) will capture the Amazon-anniversary sell-in normalisation that management itself flagged — 'the important sell-in due to the Amazon piece last year that will be equilibrated' (1 May 2026) — on top of a decelerating fragrance segment (up 3.9% like-for-like in the first quarter), a negative 4% foreign-exchange drag, and the Middle East and travel-retail headwind (roughly negative 1% for the first half, with March travel-retail reportedly down roughly 50%). That combination could produce a soft, optically weak print that, absent deal news, removes the operating cushion beneath the current price.

Second, the Estee Lauder process is itself the risky window. 'Potential business combination' with 'no final decision' and 'no assurance regarding the transaction or the terms' (1 May 2026) means a multi-month confidentiality vacuum in which the stock trades on deal-sentiment. A leak of unfavourable terms, a regulatory complication, or an outright break would pull the stock back toward its standalone discounted-cash-flow value — roughly 16 euros, essentially the current price — with no catalyst remaining.

Third, the regulatory and timing overlay: the permissive US merger-review environment is a dated opportunity. A Puig–Estee Lauder combination is a large, visible cosmetics tie-up; if it slips past the middle of 2026, the November midterm elections bring a plausible shift toward tighter antitrust scrutiny, an explicit tripwire noted in the current regime analysis. In summary, the risky window is: a soft summer earnings print, a terms-opaque deal that could break or dilute, and a closing regulatory clock — no balance-sheet danger, but a real catalyst-timing trough.
positioning_washout:
The classic forced-selling pressure question — will a dividend cut or strategy pivot force yield-focused or index-benchmark holders to liquidate mechanically — does not apply cleanly here. Puig pays only a modest 40% dividend payout ratio (its first dividend was paid in June 2025) and is not a yield vehicle, so there is no income-investor base to flush out. The relevant forced-flow dynamics are different and mostly latent rather than imminent.

Dual-class and free-float mechanics dominate the tape. The founding Puig family controls the company through Class A shares, while only the Class B free-float shares were offered in the IPO. The smart-money positioning score (26 out of 100), institutional flow score (50 out of 100), and insider buy ratio of 0.00 indicate neither the family nor institutions are adding through open-market purchases. Family buying is structurally unlikely given their control stake, so the zero insider buy ratio is not itself a bearish signal — it is expected.

The thin Class B free float means the stock can move sharply on deal headlines with relatively little fundamental selling required. The genuine mechanical event embedded in this situation is the merger structure itself: if the Estee Lauder combination is completed as a stock-for-stock merger, index-membership treatment, the surviving listing domicile, and any change in Puig's weight in the Spanish IBEX or the Euro Stoxx indices would force price-insensitive passive-fund reconstitution flows in or out — potentially significant and entirely indifferent to fundamental value.

Conversely, a deal break is the forced-selling pressure event that matters most here. Merger-arbitrage funds and event-driven holders who entered on the March 2026 confirmation would liquidate mechanically on a break — and that flush, not a dividend cut, is the specific capitulation moment a new investor should monitor and consider buying. No broader forced de-grossing is tied to oil prices or technology-capital-expenditure flows, because Puig is structurally uncorrelated to those themes — a genuine portfolio-construction benefit, offset only by the modest Middle East travel-retail demand drag. In summary, no imminent mechanical sell is approaching, but a binary one is embedded in the merger outcome: a break-driven event-holder flush is the cleanest potential entry trigger.
moderator_conclusion:
Wait for the wash-out, conviction 3. Puig is a genuinely high-quality business — management credibility 4 of 5, eight consecutive quarters of market outperformance, EBITDA margin expanding to 20.7%, trailing twelve months free cash flow of 1.28 billion euros (up 38% year on year), net leverage of 0.7 times, a 67% earnings beat rate, and a management team that is transparently candid with its investors. This is not a value trap in the financial-distress sense.

But the deep-value classification is the weakest part of the argument. The screener's 332% margin of safety to a 64 euro intrinsic value is undermined by an unreconciled dual-class share-count contradiction — 1.04 euros adjusted earnings per share on the full share base versus 1.99 euros trailing-twelve-months diluted on the free-float denominator — and a discounted-cash-flow fair value of 16.2 euros that sits essentially at the current price. Earnings power value (the no-growth valuation) shows a margin of safety of negative 100%, meaning the no-growth case does not even support the current price. The fundamental 'cheapness' is largely a measurement artifact rather than a genuine opportunity.

With the operating core decelerating — Fragrance and Fashion up 3.9% like-for-like in the first quarter, group up 4.7%, the slowest in the dataset — headline growth repeatedly flattered by sell-in timing effects (Amazon roughly half of third-quarter 2025 makeup growth; APAC consolidation uplift flagged as non-repeating; an explicit second-quarter 2026 normalisation air-pocket), value market share slipping from 11.5% to 11.1%, gross margin contracting, and a foreign-exchange headwind plus Middle East travel-retail drag, the standalone fundamental case does not offer the asymmetric deep-value entry the headline implies.

What saves it from a pass, and what the current investment regime actively rewards, is the live, hard-dated Estee Lauder potential business combination — confirmed 23 March 2026, still ongoing as of 1 May 2026 — executed alongside a deliberate chief-executive succession and a Capital Markets Day postponement. This is a structural special situation in the merger-and-acquisition lane, landing while the US and EU regulatory window is open and permissive.

Three considerations keep conviction at 3 and the recommendation at 'wait' rather than 'buy now'. First, the forcing function is a single binary node: everything rides on one Estee Lauder outcome, not a stack of independent catalysts. The 'potential combination / merge their businesses' language implies a stock-for-stock deal that may re-rate Puig only to a combined-entity multiple plus contested synergy value rather than delivering a clean cash premium. Second, the regulatory window is time-boxed: permissive conditions may narrow after the November 2026 US midterm elections, so terms need to crystallise in the first half or summer of 2026 or the thesis becomes materially riskier. Third, the cleanest entry is after a specific catalyst, not now: either a deal-break-driven event-holder capitulation — which would expose a soft standalone but at a much better price — or the soft second-quarter/first-half 2026 earnings print (expected the week of 27 July 2026) that prices in the Amazon-anniversary normalisation.

Timing call: do not chase the deal spread at current levels. Put Puig on the watchlist and wait for either definitive Estee Lauder terms confirming a premium or accretive structure — buy the confirmed catalyst — or a deal break or soft summer flush that resets the standalone compounder to a defensible entry point.
interrogator_dossier:
Puig Brands, S.A. (PUIG.MC) — Forensic Earnings-Call Dossier

Transcript span: first half of 2024 (first call as a public company, 9 September 2024) through the first-quarter 2026 sales update (1 May 2026). Eight consecutive disclosures cross-referenced against the screener's multi-year financial trajectory. Signal type: deep value.


Narrative Arc

Puig's story across eight quarters is, on the surface, one of the most internally consistent in the entire screener universe — and that consistency is itself the central forensic fact. From its IPO debut (9 September 2024) to its full-year 2025 results (18 February 2026), the core narrative never deviated: a family-controlled, founder-led company that owns 96% of its love brands, runs three of the top-ten global prestige fragrance lines (Rabanne, Carolina Herrera, Jean Paul Gaultier), and 'outperforms the premium beauty market' through brand desirability, innovation cadence, and gross margins above 75%. Management repeated the 'outperform the premium beauty market' formulation in literally every call, and by the first-quarter 2026 update had codified it into a streak claim: 'as we have done consistently for each of the last 5 years, including each of the last 8 quarters as a public company' (1 May 2026).

The arc has three distinct chapters.

Chapter 1 — IPO confidence and the fragrance flywheel (first half 2024 through full year 2024). The emphasis was offensive: Fragrance and Fashion (73% of revenue) compounding double-digit, Jean Paul Gaultier entering the top-ten for the first time, Good Girl becoming the number-one feminine line in the US. Growth accelerated through 2024 — first half up 8.5% like-for-like, nine months up 9.6%, fourth quarter up 14.1% like-for-like (full year up 10.9%) — capped by the strategically important extension of the Charlotte Tilbury partnership to 2031 with a path to full ownership. Makeup was the one soft spot (full year 2024 down 1.3%), explained at the time as sell-in/sell-out destocking, the Christian Louboutin China drag, and a voluntary Airbrush Setting Spray withdrawal.

Chapter 2 — Deceleration acknowledged, mix rotates (first half 2025 through third quarter 2025). The narrative shifted from 'accelerating' to 'moderating but resilient.' Management proactively flagged fragrance-category deceleration: 'our best estimation of the growth of the category as a whole, it was in the mid-single-digit number… over the past couple of months, we are seeing moderation… we expect the second half to be more in the low single digit' (12 September 2025). Growth drivers rotated — Fragrance decelerated to up 2.8% like-for-like in the third quarter while Makeup reached up 18.8% (roughly half attributed to a one-time Amazon US sell-in pipeline fill) and APAC surged up 35.8% (partly a subsidiary-consolidation effect management said they did not expect to repeat). The honesty about these mechanical, non-repeatable boosts is a positive signal for management candour.

Chapter 3 — Succession executed, then the combination announcement (full year 2025 through first quarter 2026). Full-year 2025 (18 February 2026) closed the IPO chapter cleanly: 5.04 billion euros in revenue (the five-billion euro milestone), up 7.8% like-for-like at the top of the guided 6-8% range, EBITDA margin expanded to 20.7%, leverage at a trivial 0.7 times. Then the arc snaps. The planned April 2026 Capital Markets Day — at which a new multi-year vision plan was to be unveiled — was postponed, and on the first-quarter 2026 call (1 May 2026) a new chief executive, Jose Manuel Muniesa, delivered his first call and confirmed 'ongoing discussion regarding a potential business combination with Estee Lauder, in which the two companies will potentially merge their business' (originally announced 23 March 2026). The growth-compounder narrative has been overtaken, mid-stride, by a transformational corporate-event narrative.

The single most important arc observation: the operational story quietly downshifted across 2025 — fragrance category moderating, first-quarter 2026 like-for-like up only 4.7%, the slowest print in the dataset — at exactly the moment a strategic combination catalyst appeared. The 'deep value' classification is anchored on the screener's 332% margin of safety to a 64 euro fair value against a 16 euro price, and that gap now has a live, dated forcing function attached to it.


Claims Versus Financials (Forensic Check)

The financial data carries an important internal contradiction that must be understood before reading the table below. The screener's fiscal-year block shows earnings per share rising from 0.39 to 0.70 to 0.82 to 0.98 to 1.05 euros (2021 to 2025) and full-year 2025 net income of 594 million euros, while the trailing-twelve-months override block reports diluted earnings per share of 1.99 euros and free cash flow of 1.28 billion euros as of 31 December 2025. Both cannot describe the same share count on the same basis. Puig has a dual-class structure: Class A shares held by the family and Class B free-float shares issued in the IPO. The 1.04 euro 'adjusted earnings per share' management quotes (18 February 2026) reconciles to the roughly 594 million euros of net income on the full roughly 1.1 billion share base, whereas the 1.99 euro trailing-twelve-months figure appears to be computed on the free-float Class B denominator only. For valuation purposes, the director must resolve which share count the 64 euro fair value assumes — this is the difference between a stock trading at roughly 8 times and roughly 15 times earnings. This denominator ambiguity is the most material single uncertainty in the file and is not a management-credibility issue; it is a data-plumbing issue the screener must reconcile.

Claim by claim:

'Outperform the premium beauty market' every period (all eight calls). Revenue grew from 2.59 billion to 3.62 billion to 4.30 billion to 4.79 billion to 5.04 billion euros; three-year compound annual growth rate 11.7%; full-year 2025 up 7.8% like-for-like on a market management pegs at low-to-mid-single-digit. Verdict: verified. Sustained share gains are real; the company genuinely grew well above a decelerating category.

Gross margin 'one of the highest in the industry, premiumisation-driven' (first half 2024 at 75.8%, full year 2025 at 75.1%). The screener flags gross margin at 70.4% and contracting, negative 0.35 standard deviations versus historical peak. Verdict: tension. Management's reported/adjusted gross margin of roughly 75% is materially above the screener's 70.4% (likely a different cost-allocation or accounting basis). Both agree the trend is now flat to down (full year 2025 75.1% below first-half 2025 75.8%), consistent with the 'contracting' flag. Direction matches; absolute level does not.

Margin expansion delivered (full year 2025 EBITDA at 20.7% versus 20.2% in 2024). Net margin 11.8%; free-cash-flow margin 15.0%; free cash flow up 38% year on year, three-year compound annual growth rate 41.4%. Verdict: verified and then some. EBITDA-margin expansion is real; the free cash flow acceleration (up 38%, trailing-twelve-months free cash flow 1.28 billion euros) is the strongest single number in the file and validates the 'improving cash conversion' claim at 64% conversion in full year 2025.

Balance sheet 'robust… comfortably below the 2 times threshold' (first half 2025 at 1.4 times, full year 2025 at 0.7 times). The screener shows net debt of 828 million euros and flags a possible negative net debt (net cash) position, while the Altman Z-score reads 2.0. Verdict: verified on leverage, ambiguous on sign. Management's 0.7 times net-debt-to-EBITDA on roughly 1.05 billion euros of EBITDA implies roughly 700 million euros of net debt — modest either way. A conflicting 'net cash' flag in the screener appears to contradict management's own 'net debt 0.7 times' figure; treat as de minimis leverage pending reconciliation. The Altman Z-score of 2.0 looks alarming for a company with a 20% EBITDA margin and is almost certainly distorted by the dual-class market-capitalisation input — do not read it as a distress signal here.

Makeup weakness is 'sell-in/sell-out timing, not demand' (full year 2024 down 1.3%). Makeup then printed up 13.7% like-for-like in full year 2025 and up 9.2% in the first quarter of 2026. Verdict: vindicated. The destocking explanation proved correct; Makeup recovered exactly as guided. A clean, checkable credibility win.

Charlotte Tilbury 'still has legs… distributed only in roughly one-fifth of its footprint' (full year 2025 through first quarter 2026). Makeup was the fastest-growing segment in full year 2025; Charlotte Tilbury is the number-one brand in the UK and number-three in the US. Verdict: plausible, partly proven. The Amazon US and Mexico expansions delivered; the Boots UK and broader rollout are real, dated future levers. The recurring Ulta-2023 to Amazon-2025 pattern is worth noting: each distribution add front-loads a sell-in surge that later normalises — a sell-in/sell-out cycle the bears can and do model.

Fragrance category 'will still grow at 6-7%' (original IPO and market view, October 2024). By 12 September 2025, management itself cut the category forecast to 'low single digit' for the second half; first-quarter 2026 fragrance came in at up 3.9% like-for-like. Verdict: walked back. The earliest market-growth framing did not hold; to their credit, management revised it openly rather than defending an outdated view.

Net forensic read: where the numbers can be checked on a like-for-like basis, management is verified or vindicated on growth, free cash flow, leverage, and the makeup-timing call. The divergences are measurement-basis gaps (adjusted versus statutory gross margin; dual-class earnings-per-share and Altman distortion), not credibility gaps. This is a high-integrity operating story with a noisy data overlay.


Tone and Confidence Trajectory

Overall: stable to mildly declining on the operating cadence, with a hard break at first-quarter 2026 (new voice, corporate-event overhang).

First half of 2024 (offensive, high confidence): 'we are confident in the outlook'; 'we continue to outperform the market.' Marc Puig set a measured, founder's-long-view tone.

Third quarter of 2024 (peak confidence): 'retailers are being quite bullish on the fragrance category'; acceleration to up 11.6% like-for-like. The most upbeat call in the set.

Full-year 2024 sales update (confident, milestone framing): up 14.1% like-for-like in the fourth quarter, Charlotte Tilbury partnership extended to 2031 — celebratory but disciplined.

First half of 2025 (first defensive notes): 'some moderation in underlying growth, which was particularly evident in Q2'; guidance nudged to 'the lower side of the range.' The pivot from offence to candour begins. Simultaneously, the deputy chief executive appointment of Jose Manuel Albesa was announced — the first visible succession signal.

Third quarter of 2025 (recovered confidence, but narrower base): guidance raised back to the 'middle' of the range on a healthy advance order book. 'September and October have gone high, and the mood we have seen in the open-to-buy has been healthy.' Confidence restored, but now carried disproportionately by makeup and APAC one-offs rather than the fragrance core.

Full year 2025 (steady, valedictory): top-of-range delivery, margin beat. Marc Puig's last full-results call reads like a clean handoff.

First quarter of 2026 (new chief executive, guarded, corporate-event-shadowed): Jose Manuel Muniesa opened by addressing the Estee Lauder talks first, then adopted a deliberately reassuring operating tone — 'record first-quarter sales and a solid start' — over the slowest like-for-like growth in the dataset (up 4.7%) and a new geopolitical drag ('the ongoing situation in the Middle East had an estimated minus 1.2% impact'). The Capital Markets Day postponement was acknowledged with palpable regret.

The specificity of guidance increased over time — management volunteered category-growth estimates, market-share basis points, foreign-exchange basis points, Argentina hyperinflation effects to the nearest million euros — which is a credibility positive. Until the first quarter of 2026, where the corporate-combination confidentiality wall necessarily reduced forward colour. The tonal decline at that point is mostly exogenous (succession, deal silence, Middle East conflict) rather than a deterioration in the underlying business.


Guidance Credibility: High

Puig is, on this dataset, a serial guidance-deliverer. IPO 'high-single-digit like-for-like' medium-term guide delivered 10.9% like-for-like in full year 2024 (beat). Full-year 2025 '6-8% like-for-like' guide, set at IPO and reaffirmed all year, tracked from 'lower side' in September to 'middle' in October to 'top end' actual of 7.8% (18 February 2026). The intra-year upgrades were earned, not reversed. Full-year 2025 'EBITDA margin expansion in line with 2024' delivered 20.7% versus 20.2%, 'comfortably above guidance.' Full-year 2024 makeup 'gap will narrow' — it did, reversing to up 13.7% in 2025.

This maps cleanly to the screener's 67% earnings-per-share beat rate and the rising earnings trajectory. There is a mild sandbagging signature — guiding to the low end mid-year and finishing at the top end — which is the favourable kind of credibility pattern. The one genuine walk-back was the market-growth assumption (6-7% to low single digit), but that is a category call, not a company guide, and it was revised transparently. The only forward-guidance risk now is that the Estee Lauder process makes full-year 2026 guidance deliberately vague: the first-quarter 2026 call offered only 'outperform the market' and 'EBITDA margins broadly stable,' far thinner than the prior cadence.


Analyst Pressure Points

The question-and-answer sessions reveal a well-informed, persistent sell-side (Santander's Szachtman, CaixaBank's Rito, Barclays' Folan, Goldman Sachs' Nicolai, Alantra, Bank of America, JB Capital) circling five recurring concerns.

First, the sell-in/sell-out timing cycle — the dominant recurring worry. Analysts pressed every quarter on whether makeup growth was real demand or a retailer pipeline fill, first with Ulta in 2023, then with Amazon in 2025. Folan (30 October 2025): 'how much of a benefit the Charlotte Tilbury sell-in on Amazon was.' Management conceded 'Amazon represents about half of that 18.8% growth' — a candid admission that validated the analysts' instinct that the headline overstated underlying demand. Pannuti (30 October 2025) explicitly invoked the Ulta precedent to ask when normalisation hits. This is the bears' best-supported line of argument, and it recurs to the present day — Colaco (1 May 2026) pressed on second-quarter sell-in normalisation.

Second, fragrance-category deceleration and value market-share slippage. Rito repeatedly probed share losses; full-year 2025 confirmed value market share slipped from 11.5% to 11.1%, which management half-attributed to Latin American promotional intensity they 'didn't necessarily follow' and half to 'improved measurement.' A real, if minor, ding to the share-gain narrative.

Third, 'why don't you move faster on Charlotte Tilbury?' (Rito, 30 October 2025). Analysts see the distribution whitespace and are frustrated by the deliberate, scarcity-protecting pace. Management's answer — protect brand desirability — is strategically sound but leaves visible potential upside uncaptured: the bull case frames it as latent optionality, the bear case as under-monetisation.

Fourth, APAC growth quality. The up 35.8% third-quarter and up 21.7% full-year 2025 APAC prints drew scrutiny; management volunteered proactively that a subsidiary-consolidation uplift 'we do not expect to repeat in 2026' inflated those numbers — pre-empting the bad-news reveal honestly.

Fifth, margin phasing and advertising and promotion intensity (Folan, Szachtman). The first-half 2025 up-140-basis-point advertising and promotion spike (Million Gold US launch) compressed Fragrance segment margin from 18.6% to 17.8%; management guided it would not persist, and full-year 2025 margin expansion proved that correct.

Topics analysts pushed on that did become bad news: the makeup sell-in question (Amazon normalisation is now an explicit second-quarter 2026 headwind); the fragrance-category-growth question (downgraded to low single digit); Latin American promotional pressure (recurring drag). Topic management has not addressed and analysts will demand answers on: the terms, exchange ratio, and rationale of the Estee Lauder combination — a confidentiality black box that dominates the current information vacuum.


Strengths and Risks

Strengths: eight consecutive quarters of market outperformance, transparently evidenced through value market-share figures, top-ten brand rankings, and country-by-country sell-out share data (first-quarter 2026: up 1.4% Germany, up 1.8% France, up 1.1% Italy, up 0.2% US in makeup sell-out). Free cash flow inflection — trailing-twelve-months free cash flow 1.28 billion euros, up 38% year on year, three-year compound annual growth rate 41.4%, 64% conversion; deleveraging to 0.7 times — a cash machine, not a story stock. Management candour on non-repeatable boosts: voluntarily flagged Amazon sell-in (roughly half of makeup growth), APAC subsidiary-consolidation uplift, and Argentina hyperinflation swings to the nearest euro. Guidance over-delivery with favourable sandbagging — low-end to top-end pattern in full year 2025. Makeup recovery vindicated the 'timing not demand' thesis — a clean, checkable win. Charlotte Tilbury partnership locked to 2031 with a path to full ownership, securing the second growth engine.

Risks: the sell-in/sell-out timing cycle — recurring distribution-launch pipeline fills (Ulta to Amazon to Boots) repeatedly flatter headline growth and create predictable normalisation air-pockets; an explicit second-quarter 2026 headwind. Fragrance core decelerating — the 73%-of-revenue engine fell from up 11-14% like-for-like (2024) to up 3.9% (first quarter 2026); the growth base is rotating onto smaller, lumpier segments. Gross-margin contraction — screener flags contracting gross margin, negative 0.35 standard deviations versus peak; management's own reported gross margin moved from 75.8% in the first half 2025 to 75.1% in full year 2025. Value market-share slippage — from 11.5% to 11.1%, partially conceded as a genuine loss to promotional competitors. Governance flux at the worst moment — deputy chief executive (mid-2025), then new chief executive (first quarter 2026), then Capital Markets Day postponed, vision plan shelved, and combination talks — significant organisational change layered onto a transformational corporate event. Estee Lauder combination overhang is double-edged: it could be the forcing function for re-rating, or a deal-break/dilution risk; the confidentiality wall means investors are flying blind on terms. The insider buy ratio of 0.00 and smart-money positioning of 26 out of 100 indicate neither the family nor institutions are signalling conviction through open-market purchases, though the family's control stake makes such purchases structurally unlikely regardless.


Hidden Signals

The voice change is the loudest signal. The first-quarter 2026 call is the first not led by Marc Puig — Jose Manuel Muniesa (chief executive) and Miquel Angel Serra (chief financial officer) replace Marc Puig and Joan Albiol/Ramis. A founder chief executive stepping back from the earnings stage immediately before or during a combination with a US strategic is a textbook signal of a control event in motion. The deputy chief executive creation (12 September 2025) reads in hindsight as the first move of a deliberate succession choreography, not a routine organisational adjustment.

'Potential business combination… merge their business' — not 'be acquired,' not 'sell' — implies a merger of near-equals or a stock-for-stock combination with Estee Lauder rather than a clean cash acquisition. This matters enormously for the thesis: a stock merger re-rates Puig toward a combined-entity multiple and synergy value, but may not deliver the clean cash premium that a 332% indicated margin of safety implies.

Capital Markets Day postponement means optionality preserved. Shelving the multi-year vision plan specifically because of the deal tells you the deal is advanced enough that standalone guidance would be moot — a tell that talks are substantive, not exploratory.

Terminology drift toward 'normalisation' and 'discipline.' Across 2025, the lexicon shifted from 'acceleration, outperformance, momentum' toward 'disciplined execution, cost discipline, resilience, more normalised market environment.' Classic late-cycle defensive vocabulary, even as the absolute numbers held.

APAC reframed from afterthought to stated priority. 'APAC represents over one-third of the global beauty market yet accounts for just 11% of group sales… now is the right moment to accelerate' (1 May 2026). The incoming chief executive is planting a self-help growth flag — a useful standalone re-rating lever if the combination fails.

Capital-allocation discipline is unusually conservative for the sector — 40% dividend payout, a 2 times leverage ceiling, 'highly selective' approach to acquisitions, deleveraging to 0.7 times. A balance sheet built either to make a large acquisition or to be an attractive combination partner — which the Estee Lauder talks now contextualise.


Summary for the Portfolio Manager

Classification: trajectory turning. Puig is moving from a steadily compounding, founder-run standalone beauty platform into a transformational corporate-event situation (Estee Lauder combination), executed in lockstep with a chief-executive succession. The underlying operating engine is stable to strengthening (free cash flow inflecting, margins expanding, eight consecutive quarters of market outperformance, balance sheet pristine); but the equity thesis has been redefined by a live, dated corporate event that now dominates everything else.

The single most important thing to weigh before sizing a position: resolve the share-count and valuation basis before trusting the 332% indicated margin of safety. The deep-value classification rests on a 64 euro fair value against a 16 euro price, but the file contains an unreconciled dual-class earnings-per-share contradiction (1.04 euros adjusted versus 1.99 euros trailing-twelve-months diluted) and a distorted Altman Z-score (2.0) that both flow from the dual-class structure. If the 64 euro anchor assumes the free-float denominator while the price reflects the full share base (or vice-versa), a large portion of the '332%' is a measurement artifact, not real upside. Net of that caveat, this is a high-quality compounder trading at a reasonable valuation with a hard catalyst attached — but the catalyst is a 'potential combination' with binary terms risk, not a self-funded buyback or asset sale that Puig controls. Size to the deal's risk/reward (premium versus break versus dilutive stock merger), not to the headline indicated margin of safety.

Management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory turning; moat narrow but real; moat trend stable; secular threat manageable.

bull_thesis: Puig is a high-quality, founder-built prestige-beauty compounder the market is now mispricing as a busted-deal stock rather than the franchise it is. (1) ELITE EXECUTION CREDIBILITY: in two years public, management met or beat every self-set guide (IPO 'high-single-digit LFL' -> FY24 +10.9%, FY25 +7.8% at the top of the raised 6-8% range) and pre-warned every soft spot (makeup sell-in/out gap flagged H1-24 and closed exactly as promised; Airbrush setting-spray withdrawal disclosed/quantified/'managed'; Argentina hyperinflation quantified to EUR 36M). Interrogator credibility 4/5 -- you are buying an operator whose word is the asset. (2) THE PORTFOLIO SELF-HEALS AND BROADENS: makeup went from FY24 problem child (-1.3%) to FY25 fastest grower (+13.7% LFL, Q4 +26.5%) on Charlotte Tilbury; niche (Byredo, double-digit every quarter) and APAC (+21.7% FY25, +26.1% Q1-26) are durable new legs off tiny bases (Tilbury 'distributed only in ~1/5 of the footprint it could be in'; Puig the least-APAC-penetrated peer into a 35%-of-global category). Three top-10 global prestige fragrance brands, 96% owned. (3) CLEAN CASH MACHINE: EBITDA margin 20.2%->20.7% while deleveraging to 0.7x; TTM FCF EUR 1.28B at 64% conversion; ROE 28.6%. At ~EUR 16.3 (P/FCF ~11x) the stock sits below its 50-day average and ~14% under its pre-deal level -- the market threw out the takeout premium AND part of the franchise. Segment SoP on luxury-beauty multiples and an EV/EBITDA cross-check point to EUR 20-27 standalone, i.e., the franchise with NO deal supports 25-65% upside, with residual M&A interest a free option on top. CONDITIONS: LFL stabilizes mid-single-digit (not outright decline as the fragrance super-cycle cools); new CEO/CFO execute as the founder did; 20.7% margin holds vs tariffs/FX; the deal-break overhang clears over 2-3 quarters.
bear_thesis: Strip the dead deal away and this is a decelerating, FX-battered, fairly-priced beauty stock mid-management-reset, and the scan's cheapness is an illusion. (1) THE ONLY HARD CATALYST IS DEAD: the Estee Lauder combination -- the lone event capable of a step-change re-rate -- was TERMINATED 2026-05-21 on a valuation gap, controlling-family disagreement, and the Charlotte Tilbury change-of-control clause; the stock fell ~14% and has not recovered. The break reasons are STRUCTURAL, not price-only (Tilbury clause + dual-family/dual-class control are permanent obstacles to ANY sale), so 'a suitor returns' is soft optionality, not a thesis. (2) GROWTH IS ROLLING OVER: LFL FY24 +10.9% -> FY25 +7.8% -> Q1-26 +4.7%, reported +0.8% after -4% FX; management's own new lexicon ('market moderation,' 'more moderate consumer environment,' 'normalized from previously elevated levels,' 'softer trend in prestige') is a deflating post-COVID fragrance boom, and Puig is the most fragrance-exposed peer (74% of Q1-26 sales). Value market share already slipped 11.5%->11.1% (FY25, first crack in the 20-yr share-gain story); Rabanne, the largest brand, is 'softer.' (3) THINNER QUALITY THAN THE HEADLINE: ROIC 7.0% vs ROE 28.6% -- the gap is acquisition goodwill (Byredo/Tilbury/Sturm); statutory gross margin 70.4% is flagged CONTRACTING (-0.35 sigma vs peak) under tariff+FX+mix even as 'record' adjusted margin is trumpeted. A fragrance house at L'Oreal-like EV/Sales but ~half L'Oreal's net margin and 7% ROIC does not deserve L'Oreal's multiple -- the bull's sales multiples are unearned. (4) CORRUPT SCAN VALUATION: the 332% MoS / EUR 64 'Buffett' fair value extrapolates 28% historical EPS CAGR into perpetuity (absurd for a EUR 5B house decelerating to mid-single-digit LFL); honest DCF EUR 16.2 (~spot), EPV MoS -100%; the FMP 'TTM diluted EPS 1.99' is distorted (company adjusted EPS EUR 1.04) and must not anchor a P/E. (5) MANAGEMENT RESET AT THE WORST TIME: simultaneous new CEO and CFO with one public call of record, having just failed to deliver the biggest event in the company's history and pulled their own first Capital Markets Day. CONFIRMS THE BEAR: Q2/H1-26 (week of Jul 27) LFL below +4% or an FY downgrade; further gross-margin compression; no renewed bid and a drift toward the EUR 13-14 52-week low.
consensus_delta: There is NO favorable expectations gap for this strategy -- if anything the street is slightly too optimistic. Sell-side consensus is 'Buy' with a ~EUR 19.28 average 12-mo target (10 buy / 6 hold / 1 sell, ~+30% implied) and a EUR 26.6 high -- analysts are anchoring on the pre-deal quality-compounder framing and have not fully re-based for (a) the deal's STRUCTURAL death (Tilbury clause + dual-family control block re-approach), (b) LFL deceleration to +4.7% with management's own 'market moderation / softer prestige' language, and (c) statutory gross-margin contraction under tariffs/FX. The scan's 332% MoS / EUR 64 'Buffett' fair value is the most-wrong number in the file -- it extrapolates 28% historical EPS CAGR into perpetuity; the honest DCF is ~spot and EPV MoS is -100%. forcing_function: There is no positive forcing function live -- the one that existed (the Estee Lauder combination) already fired and failed on 2026-05-21. The next DATED events are earnings/governance, not re-rate catalysts: Annual General Meeting 2026-05-29 (passed); Q2/H1-2026 results the week of 2026-07-27 (the key near-term print, skewed to DOWNSIDE on the softer-Q1 guide, FX, and Middle East); and the indefinitely-POSTPONED Capital Markets Day, whose eventual rescheduling would be the clearest sign management has moved on from deal limbo and refocused on standalone strategy (a potential MILD positive, undated). valley_of_death: Concrete 3-9 month hazards that hit before any (soft) re-rate: (1) Q2/H1-2026 results (week of 2026-07-27) -- the first clean read post-break. Management already guided a 'softer Q1' and flagged a Middle East / travel-retail drag (Q1 group -1.2%, ~EUR 8M in March, '~1% for H1') plus a continuing FX headwind ('-3.6% in Q4… expect to continue into 2026', Q1 reported only +0.8%). A sub-+4% LFL print or any FY guide trim would confirm the bear and pressure the multiple. (3) Gross-margin compression: statutory 70.4% already flagged contracting; tariffs (>95% of product is EU-manufactured and imported into the US), glass/input-cost inflation, and Middle East travel-retail softness all bear on the 20.7% EBITDA-margin guide. (4) Fragrance super-cycle deflation -- the category is 'normalizing from previously elevated levels' and Puig is 74% fragrance. None of these is a balance-sheet/solvency event (0.7x leverage, EUR 1.28B FCF -- no maturity wall, no cash-burn hump), but each is a NEAR-TERM earnings/sentiment headwind with no offsetting hard catalyst to absorb it; the path of least resistance into late-July is sideways-to-down toward the EUR 13-14 low.
positioning_washout: Limited classic forced-selling, but a real residual event-driven overhang. Puig pays a dividend and is family/dual-class controlled (the Puig family retains voting control via unlisted Class A), so there is no yield-base or index-cut washout mechanism to exploit. The relevant flow already largely PLAYED OUT: the ~14% drop on 2026-05-22 was the merger-arb / event-driven base liquidating the (now dead) deal. The residual risk is a SLOW bleed rather than a flush -- event-driven and 'lucky-escape'-relieved holders trimming, plus quality/GARP funds re-rating the name down as LFL decelerates and the new CEO/CFO are de-risked. Because the sharp mechanical sell is behind us and what remains is gradual, there is no capitulation flush to buy -- which is itself a reason NOT to wait for one (verdict B is not warranted) and to simply PASS. moderator_conclusion: PASS (C), conviction 2. Puig is a genuinely good BUSINESS wrapped in a dead SITUATION, and this strategy underwrites situations. The franchise is high-quality -- elite guidance credibility (4/5), a self-healing portfolio (makeup -1.3%->+13.7%), durable niche/APAC legs, 20.7% EBITDA margin, 0.7x leverage, EUR 1.28B FCF -- and on generous luxury-beauty EV/Sales SoP it 'looks' worth EUR 30+. But two disciplines kill the trade. FIRST, the load-bearing catalyst is FIRED: the Estee Lauder combination was terminated 2026-05-21 on STRUCTURAL grounds (Tilbury change-of-control clause, dual-family control, valuation gap), the stock fell ~14% and sits below its 50-day average, and a re-approach is soft single-binary optionality, not a hard event -- a FIRED catalyst is not an asymmetric special-sit, it is at best defensive/arb with nothing left to arb. REGIME OVERLAY (2026-06-05 read) seals it: the regime explicitly PENALIZES past/out-of-window catalysts and single-shared-binary theses, and rewards STRUCTURAL special-sits in thin-coverage lanes (distressed/deleveraging > spinoffs > forced-sellers). Puig is the opposite -- a large-cap, 17-analyst-covered consumer compounder whose one event already failed, with merger-arb itself the regime's thinnest/compressing lane and no live deal even present. Risk/reward on the standalone is only ~1.2-1.5:1 (+23% to base vs -15-20% to the EUR 13-14 low) and is contingent on a slow re-rate with NO forcing function; the next dated event (H1 results, week of Jul 27) skews DOWN on the softer-Q1/FX/Middle-East guide and the Tilbury Amazon-anniversary makeup air-pocket. interrogator_dossier:
# Puig Brands S.A. (PUIG.MC) — Forensic Interrogator Dossier
*Prepared by the Interrogator, Investment Committee — capital-at-risk review. Transcripts: H1-2024 (first call as a public company, 2024-09-09) through Q1-2026 (2026-05-01). Financials: screener scan + FMP TTM through 2025-12-31.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

Puig came public in May 2024 with a tight, repeatable story and has told essentially the same story every quarter since — which is itself the most important fact about this name. The core pitch from the very first call (2024-09-09): a **"portfolio of Love Brands"** spanning three segments (fragrance & fashion, makeup, skincare), where **"our own brands represent 96% of the net revenues"** (i.e., low licence risk), anchored by three top-10 global prestige fragrance brands (Rabanne, Carolina Herrera, Jean Paul Gaultier), guided to **"high single-digit like-for-like growth"** medium-term and a beauty market the company insists **"will still grow at 6%, 7%"** (2024-10-29) even as peers cut their market view.

The arc is one of **consistent over-delivery against a deliberately conservative frame, then a single transformational event that overwhelmed the operating story**:
- **2024 (H1 → Q3 → FY):** sequential *acceleration* — H1 LFL +8.5%, 9M +9.6%, FY +10.9%, with Q4 +14.1% — every quarter beat the prior. Fragrance was the engine (FY24 segment +13.6%); makeup was the persistent sore spot (FY24 −1.3%, dragged by Christian Louboutin/China, Charlotte Tilbury destock, and the Airbrush Flawless setting-spray voluntary withdrawal).
- **2025 (FY, reported 2026-02-18):** the story *broadened and de-risked* — **makeup flipped from the problem child to the fastest grower (+13.7% LFL)**, niche fragrance (Byredo) and APAC (+21.7% LFL) became the new growth legs, EBITDA margin expanded to 20.7%, leverage fell to 0.7x. FY25 LFL +7.8% landed at the *top* of the 6–8% guide. A textbook "operationally everything is working" year.
- **Q1-2026 (2026-05-01):** two regime changes at once. (1) A **new CEO, Jose Manuel Muniesa, delivered his first call** — Marc Puig has stepped from CEO to Chairman. (2) The call opened not with results but with confirmation of **"ongoing discussion regarding a potential business combination with The Estée Lauder"** (first disclosed 2026-03-23). Operating growth visibly *decelerated* (group LFL +4.7%, fragrance +3.9%) into "market moderation," a −4% FX drag, and a Middle East/travel-retail hit (−1.2% to group, March down ~50% in the region). The Capital Markets Day (April 14) was **postponed on account of the deal talks**.

The throughline that was *introduced and then became the whole thesis*: M&A optionality. Management always reserved the right to be acquisitive ("M&A has been an integral part of our growth," 2026-02-18) — but by Q1-2026 the company had itself become the *target*, not the acquirer. **That is the load-bearing fact for any capital decision today, and as of this writing it has already resolved (see §6/§8).**

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | The hard numbers | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "High single-digit like-for-like growth" medium-term guide | IPO / 2024-09-09 | FY24 LFL **+10.9%**, FY25 LFL **+7.8%** (reported +5.3% after −2.6% FX) | **Delivered / over-delivered.** Two-for-two above or at the top of the frame. Reported growth, however, is materially lower than LFL because FX is a real, persistent drag — the scan's "+5.3% YoY" is the *reported* FY25 number, and it understates the underlying engine. |
| Record gross margin, premiumization working | every call | Scan gross margin **70.4% (flagged contracting)**; mgmt cites **75.8% H1-24 → 75.1% FY25** | **Mostly corroborated, with a wrinkle.** Mgmt's 75% is *adjusted/segment* gross margin; the scan's 70.4% is the statutory figure and it is flagged *contracting*. Both can be true — but the "record gross margin" drumbeat is richer than the statutory line, which is drifting down ~ on mix/tariff/FX. Margin sits −0.35σ vs historical peak per the scan. |
| EBITDA margin expansion, "comfortably above guidance" | 2026-02-18 | Adj. EBITDA **20.7%** (vs 20.2% FY24); net margin **11.8%** | **Corroborated.** Operating leverage is real; net margin 11.8% and FCF margin 15.0% are solid for a recently-IPO'd brand house. ## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: RISING through FY25, then ABRUPTLY GUARDED in Q1-2026 (CEO transition + live deal).**

- **2024-09-09 (Rising, measured):** confident but careful — *"we're pleased to report a robust first half,"* repeatedly hedging makeup (*"early days,"* *"we'll not rush"*). Defensive only on makeup/China.
- **2024-10-29 (Rising):** *"pleased to report an acceleration in Q3 versus first half,"* retailers *"quite bullish on the fragrance category."* Offensive language dominates.
- **2025-01-30 / 2026-02-18 (Peak confidence):** *"2025 was another year of strong delivery,"* *"comfortably above our guidance,"* *"impressive improvement."* Marc Puig is expansive on Jean Paul Gaultier's Gen-Z capture, candid about Rabanne being *"softer"* — the candor itself reads as confidence (a weak operator hides soft brands).
- **2026-05-01 (Guarded / transitional):** the tone shifts hard. New CEO Muniesa opens with a *scripted, lawyered* deal statement (*"unless an agreement is reached, there can be no assurance… we cannot comment further"*), then a Q1 that is *"a solid start"* but visibly decelerating, with new defensive vocabulary: *"market moderation,"* *"more moderate consumer environment,"* *"softer trend in prestige fragrances,"* a Middle East *"minus 1.2% impact,"* and a *"softer Q1… confident on our overall year."* The shift from offensive (share-gain, launches) to defensive (FX, macro, cost discipline, *"EBITDA margins will remain stable"*) is unmistakable.

The CEO handoff matters for tone-reading: Muniesa is competent and on-message, but the founder's storytelling swagger (the Gaultier/Gen-Z narrative, the 20-year share-gain arc) is gone, replaced by an operator's "disciplined execution" register — appropriate while a transformational deal is live, but it removes a source of narrative premium.

**Reliability: HIGH.** This is the strongest part of the file.

- IPO guide (high-single-digit LFL) → **FY24 +10.9%** (beat) → **FY25 +7.8%** (top of the explicitly-raised 6–8% range). Two clean years of meeting or beating the number they set.
- They *pre-warned* every soft spot before it hit: makeup sell-in/sell-out gap (flagged H1-24, narrowed Q3-24 exactly as promised); the Ulta pipeline tough comp ("not insignificant… particularly Makeup and skincare in Q4"); the Airbrush setting-spray withdrawal (disclosed, quantified mid-single-digit, then "managed… reshipping"); the Argentina hyperinflation swing (quantified to **€36M**, 2024-10-29). When they say a comp will be tough, it is; when they say a gap will close, it does.
- FY25 EBITDA margin "comfortably above guidance" and explicitly Q1-26 framed *down* ("softer Q1") — i.e., they sandbag the near term and then clear it.

There is a **mild sandbagging pattern** (the 6–8% guide they then top), which on the scan shows as a 67% beat rate (2/3 quarters). For a name where the *operator's word is the asset*, HIGH reliability is well-earned. The only credibility shadow is structural, not managerial: the new CEO has one call of track record, and the company just spent two months in a deal process it could not deliver.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

Recurring analyst worries, and how management handled them:
1. **Makeup / Charlotte Tilbury sell-in vs sell-out** (every call, H1-24 onward): the single most-pushed topic. Management was consistently transparent and ultimately *right* (the gap closed; makeup became the FY25 star). But Q1-26 flags it *again* — *"this balance between sell-in and sell-out that will happen in the second quarter"* on the Amazon anniversary — so the issue is recurring, not retired.
2. **Market share "loss" 11.5%→11.1% (FY25):** Tom (Q4-25 call) pushed directly. Management's answer — *"we have lost a little less than what this 0.4% gap shows… very aggressive promotional activity from some of our peers… which we didn't necessarily follow"* — is a *plausible but defensive* non-denial. First time in the file the 20-year share-gain story shows a crack.
3. **Rabanne losing momentum** (David/CIC, FY25): management conceded *"Rabanne has been softer,"* leaning on portfolio diversification. Honest, but the largest brand decelerating is a real watch-item.
4. **China/APAC under-exposure:** framed as opportunity (*"the company that has less penetration in APAC"*), and FY25/Q1-26 APAC +21.7%/+26.1% vindicated the bull read — though management flagged the FY25 APAC number was *inflated by subsidiary consolidation* "we do not expect to repeat," a quiet quality-of-growth caveat.
5. **The Estée Lauder deal (Q1-26):** every analyst wanted detail; management stonewalled by design. The *postponed Capital Markets Day* is the loud tell — they pulled their own flagship investor event because the deal made forward guidance impossible.

Pattern: management does **not** evade operational questions (rare and creditable). The only true non-answers are legally-mandated (the deal) and the one defensive share-loss explanation.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Vocabulary shift Q1-26:** "market moderation," "more moderate consumer environment," "normalized from previously elevated levels," "softer trend in prestige" — this is the language of a *category coming off a post-COVID fragrance boom*, not of a structural grower. Management euphemizes it, but the fragrance super-cycle is visibly cooling, and Puig is most exposed to it (74% of Q1-26 sales).
- **The postponed Capital Markets Day** (April 14 → indefinite) is the clearest non-verbal signal in the file: you do not pull your first investor-day-as-public-company unless something binding is in motion.
- **"We will stay active on M&A" (Marc Puig, FY25)** reads very differently in hindsight — within ~5 weeks of that call the company was confirmed in talks to be *acquired by* Estée Lauder. The acquirer-framing was, at minimum, incomplete.
- **Charlotte Tilbury ownership mechanics:** Puig "will progressively assume full ownership until the beginning of 2031" (FY24 call) — and Tilbury's residual stake + change-of-control rights became a named deal-breaker. The crown-jewel brand carries embedded minority-holder friction.
- **New CFO too:** Q1-26 introduces Miquel Angel Serra as CFO (Joan Ramis/Albiol had been CFO across prior calls). A *simultaneous* CEO and CFO change is a fuller management reset than a routine succession — worth confirming the bench is intact.
- **APAC growth quality caveat:** management volunteered that FY25 APAC was lifted by subsidiary consolidation "we do not expect to repeat" — a subtle admission that the headline +21.7% overstates organic APAC momentum.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: STABLE (operationally STRENGTHENING) business with a PIVOTING / RESET corporate-event situation.**

Puig is, on the evidence, a genuinely high-quality, well-managed prestige-beauty compounder with elite guidance credibility, real margin expansion, a clean balance sheet (~0.7x), and strong FCF (€1.28B TTM). The operating trajectory is *strengthening* (makeup fixed, niche/APAC added). If this were purely an operating-quality question, it would screen as a buy-the-quality name.

But the file's defining fact is a corporate event, and **the director must weigh one thing above all else:**

> **The load-bearing catalyst is dead.** The Estée Lauder business combination — the only event in this file capable of a step-change re-rate — was **terminated on 2026-05-21**, ~2.5 weeks before this review, on a valuation gap, controlling-family disagreement, and the Charlotte Tilbury change-of-control clause. The stock fell ~14% on the break and trades ~€16.3 today, *below* its 50-day average — i.e., the market is still digesting the disappointment, and the price already reflects the *absence* of a takeout. There is no live deal premium to capture; what remains is a standalone, decelerating (LFL +10.9%→+7.8%→+4.7%) compounder under a brand-new CEO/CFO, priced at ~11x P/FCF — reasonable but not a screaming bargain once the corrupt 332%-MoS model is discarded.

Before sizing, the director must decide whether they are underwriting **(a)** a *standalone quality compounder* at a fair-to-modestly-cheap multiple with a cooling fragrance cycle and an unproven new management team (a defensible 2–3% position on quality), or **(b)** a *renewed M&A target* betting Estée Lauder or another suitor returns — a thesis that just failed once on structural (not price-only) obstacles (Tilbury clause, dual-family control) and should be treated as SOFT optionality, not a hard catalyst. The scan's catalyst score of 0.5 and the dead deal both argue against (b). **Do not size this on the takeout; size it, if at all, on the standalone franchise — and haircut for decelerating growth, the management reset, and a fragrance super-cycle that management itself now calls "moderated."**

Sanity-check: the EV/Sales SoP that lifts value to EUR 30+ implicitly grants Puig L'Oreal-class multiples it does not yet earn; disciplining to the peer-appropriate margin tier pulls fair value back to ~EUR 20. So 'cheap vs peers' supports a HOLD/modest-discount read, not an aggressive-entry mispricing.
