# Puig Brands S.A. (PUIG.MC) — Forensic Interrogator Dossier
*Prepared by the Interrogator, Investment Committee — capital-at-risk review. Transcripts: H1-2024 (first call as a public company, 2024-09-09) through Q1-2026 (2026-05-01). Financials: screener scan + FMP TTM through 2025-12-31.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

Puig came public in May 2024 with a tight, repeatable story and has told essentially the same story every quarter since — which is itself the most important fact about this name. The core pitch from the very first call (2024-09-09): a **"portfolio of Love Brands"** spanning three segments (fragrance & fashion, makeup, skincare), where **"our own brands represent 96% of the net revenues"** (i.e., low licence risk), anchored by three top-10 global prestige fragrance brands (Rabanne, Carolina Herrera, Jean Paul Gaultier), guided to **"high single-digit like-for-like growth"** medium-term and a beauty market the company insists **"will still grow at 6%, 7%"** (2024-10-29) even as peers cut their market view.

The arc is one of **consistent over-delivery against a deliberately conservative frame, then a single transformational event that overwhelmed the operating story**:
- **2024 (H1 → Q3 → FY):** sequential *acceleration* — H1 LFL +8.5%, 9M +9.6%, FY +10.9%, with Q4 +14.1% — every quarter beat the prior. Fragrance was the engine (FY24 segment +13.6%); makeup was the persistent sore spot (FY24 −1.3%, dragged by Christian Louboutin/China, Charlotte Tilbury destock, and the Airbrush Flawless setting-spray voluntary withdrawal).
- **2025 (FY, reported 2026-02-18):** the story *broadened and de-risked* — **makeup flipped from the problem child to the fastest grower (+13.7% LFL)**, niche fragrance (Byredo) and APAC (+21.7% LFL) became the new growth legs, EBITDA margin expanded to 20.7%, leverage fell to 0.7x. FY25 LFL +7.8% landed at the *top* of the 6–8% guide. A textbook "operationally everything is working" year.
- **Q1-2026 (2026-05-01):** two regime changes at once. (1) A **new CEO, Jose Manuel Muniesa, delivered his first call** — Marc Puig has stepped from CEO to Chairman. (2) The call opened not with results but with confirmation of **"ongoing discussion regarding a potential business combination with The Estée Lauder"** (first disclosed 2026-03-23). Operating growth visibly *decelerated* (group LFL +4.7%, fragrance +3.9%) into "market moderation," a −4% FX drag, and a Middle East/travel-retail hit (−1.2% to group, March down ~50% in the region). The Capital Markets Day (April 14) was **postponed on account of the deal talks**.

The throughline that was *introduced and then became the whole thesis*: M&A optionality. Management always reserved the right to be acquisitive ("M&A has been an integral part of our growth," 2026-02-18) — but by Q1-2026 the company had itself become the *target*, not the acquirer. **That is the load-bearing fact for any capital decision today, and as of this writing it has already resolved (see §6/§8).**

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | The hard numbers | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "High single-digit like-for-like growth" medium-term guide | IPO / 2024-09-09 | FY24 LFL **+10.9%**, FY25 LFL **+7.8%** (reported +5.3% after −2.6% FX) | **Delivered / over-delivered.** Two-for-two above or at the top of the frame. Reported growth, however, is materially lower than LFL because FX is a real, persistent drag — the scan's "+5.3% YoY" is the *reported* FY25 number, and it understates the underlying engine. |
| Record gross margin, premiumization working | every call | Scan gross margin **70.4% (flagged contracting)**; mgmt cites **75.8% H1-24 → 75.1% FY25** | **Mostly corroborated, with a wrinkle.** Mgmt's 75% is *adjusted/segment* gross margin; the scan's 70.4% is the statutory figure and it is flagged *contracting*. Both can be true — but the "record gross margin" drumbeat is richer than the statutory line, which is drifting down ~ on mix/tariff/FX. Margin sits −0.35σ vs historical peak per the scan. |
| EBITDA margin expansion, "comfortably above guidance" | 2026-02-18 | Adj. EBITDA **20.7%** (vs 20.2% FY24); net margin **11.8%** | **Corroborated.** Operating leverage is real; net margin 11.8% and FCF margin 15.0% are solid for a recently-IPO'd brand house. ROE 28.6% is genuinely high; **ROIC 7.0% is the tell** — goodwill from Byredo/Tilbury/Sturm acquisitions sits heavy on invested capital, so the *returns-on-capital* story is far less impressive than the returns-on-equity headline. |
| Deleveraging, balance-sheet strength | 2026-02-18 | Leverage **0.7x net debt/EBITDA**; scan net debt 828M; live correction **net debt ~€1.05B** | **Corroborated as low-leverage.** NOTE: the input annotates the €1.05B as "[NEGATIVE = net cash]" — that is internally inconsistent (€1.05B is *positive* net debt and matches 0.7x × ~€1.05B adj-EBITDA). Treat Puig as **modestly levered (~0.7x), not net cash.** Either way the balance sheet is clean and not a constraint. |
| FCF strength | scan/TTM | Scan FCF margin 15.0%, **+38% YoY**; **TTM FCF €1.28B**; FCF conversion 64% (mgmt) | **Corroborated and arguably the best line in the file.** TTM FCF €1.28B against a ~€9.2B listed market cap is a ~14% FCF yield on the *listed* share class — though see the share-class caveat in §8 (this overstates the yield on total enterprise). |
| "Outperform the premium beauty market… each of the last 8 quarters" | 2026-05-01 | Multi-year: rev €2.59B(21)→€5.04B(25); EPS €0.39→€1.05; **EPS 5y CAGR +28.1%, BVPS +39.4%** | **Corroborated on trajectory.** The compounding is real and consistent. But the *rate* is decelerating exactly as the story matures — Q1-26 LFL +4.7% is roughly half the FY24 +10.9% peak. The "outperform the market" claim survives; the "high-growth" claim is softening to "GDP-plus." |
| Valuation: "Fair Value 64.16 / MoS 332%" (scan) | scan | DCF fair value **16.2** (≈ price); Buffett FV 64.2; EPV MoS **−100%**; Graham +63% | **The 332% MoS is a model artifact and should be discarded.** The Buffett/owner-earnings model is extrapolating 28% historical EPS CAGR into perpetuity — not credible for a maturing €5B beauty house decelerating to mid-single-digit LFL. The DCF (16.2 ≈ spot) and EPV (−100%, i.e., no margin of safety on current earnings power) are the honest anchors. **This is NOT a 332%-undervalued stock; it is roughly fairly-to-modestly-cheaply priced on cash flow (P/FCF 11x), with the cheapness driven by the deal-break overhang.** |

**Net:** the operating financials genuinely *corroborate* management's competence and consistency — this is a high-quality, well-run compounder. The divergence is not management-vs-reality (they deliver); it is **scan-model-vs-reality** (the 332% MoS and €64 fair value are nonsense) and **LFL-vs-reported** (FX makes the headline growth look weaker than the franchise).

---

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: RISING through FY25, then ABRUPTLY GUARDED in Q1-2026 (CEO transition + live deal).**

- **2024-09-09 (Rising, measured):** confident but careful — *"we're pleased to report a robust first half,"* repeatedly hedging makeup (*"early days,"* *"we'll not rush"*). Defensive only on makeup/China.
- **2024-10-29 (Rising):** *"pleased to report an acceleration in Q3 versus first half,"* retailers *"quite bullish on the fragrance category."* Offensive language dominates.
- **2025-01-30 / 2026-02-18 (Peak confidence):** *"2025 was another year of strong delivery,"* *"comfortably above our guidance,"* *"impressive improvement."* Marc Puig is expansive on Jean Paul Gaultier's Gen-Z capture, candid about Rabanne being *"softer"* — the candor itself reads as confidence (a weak operator hides soft brands).
- **2026-05-01 (Guarded / transitional):** the tone shifts hard. New CEO Muniesa opens with a *scripted, lawyered* deal statement (*"unless an agreement is reached, there can be no assurance… we cannot comment further"*), then a Q1 that is *"a solid start"* but visibly decelerating, with new defensive vocabulary: *"market moderation,"* *"more moderate consumer environment,"* *"softer trend in prestige fragrances,"* a Middle East *"minus 1.2% impact,"* and a *"softer Q1… confident on our overall year."* The shift from offensive (share-gain, launches) to defensive (FX, macro, cost discipline, *"EBITDA margins will remain stable"*) is unmistakable.

The CEO handoff matters for tone-reading: Muniesa is competent and on-message, but the founder's storytelling swagger (the Gaultier/Gen-Z narrative, the 20-year share-gain arc) is gone, replaced by an operator's "disciplined execution" register — appropriate while a transformational deal is live, but it removes a source of narrative premium.

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: HIGH.** This is the strongest part of the file.

- IPO guide (high-single-digit LFL) → **FY24 +10.9%** (beat) → **FY25 +7.8%** (top of the explicitly-raised 6–8% range). Two clean years of meeting or beating the number they set.
- They *pre-warned* every soft spot before it hit: makeup sell-in/sell-out gap (flagged H1-24, narrowed Q3-24 exactly as promised); the Ulta pipeline tough comp ("not insignificant… particularly Makeup and skincare in Q4"); the Airbrush setting-spray withdrawal (disclosed, quantified mid-single-digit, then "managed… reshipping"); the Argentina hyperinflation swing (quantified to **€36M**, 2024-10-29). When they say a comp will be tough, it is; when they say a gap will close, it does.
- FY25 EBITDA margin "comfortably above guidance" and explicitly Q1-26 framed *down* ("softer Q1") — i.e., they sandbag the near term and then clear it.

There is a **mild sandbagging pattern** (the 6–8% guide they then top), which on the scan shows as a 67% beat rate (2/3 quarters). For a name where the *operator's word is the asset*, HIGH reliability is well-earned. The only credibility shadow is structural, not managerial: the new CEO has one call of track record, and the company just spent two months in a deal process it could not deliver.

---

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

Recurring analyst worries, and how management handled them:
1. **Makeup / Charlotte Tilbury sell-in vs sell-out** (every call, H1-24 onward): the single most-pushed topic. Management was consistently transparent and ultimately *right* (the gap closed; makeup became the FY25 star). But Q1-26 flags it *again* — *"this balance between sell-in and sell-out that will happen in the second quarter"* on the Amazon anniversary — so the issue is recurring, not retired.
2. **Market share "loss" 11.5%→11.1% (FY25):** Tom (Q4-25 call) pushed directly. Management's answer — *"we have lost a little less than what this 0.4% gap shows… very aggressive promotional activity from some of our peers… which we didn't necessarily follow"* — is a *plausible but defensive* non-denial. First time in the file the 20-year share-gain story shows a crack.
3. **Rabanne losing momentum** (David/CIC, FY25): management conceded *"Rabanne has been softer,"* leaning on portfolio diversification. Honest, but the largest brand decelerating is a real watch-item.
4. **China/APAC under-exposure:** framed as opportunity (*"the company that has less penetration in APAC"*), and FY25/Q1-26 APAC +21.7%/+26.1% vindicated the bull read — though management flagged the FY25 APAC number was *inflated by subsidiary consolidation* "we do not expect to repeat," a quiet quality-of-growth caveat.
5. **The Estée Lauder deal (Q1-26):** every analyst wanted detail; management stonewalled by design. The *postponed Capital Markets Day* is the loud tell — they pulled their own flagship investor event because the deal made forward guidance impossible.

Pattern: management does **not** evade operational questions (rare and creditable). The only true non-answers are legally-mandated (the deal) and the one defensive share-loss explanation.

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🟢 **Green:**
- Two consecutive years of meeting/beating self-set guidance with pre-warned soft spots (§4) — elite execution-credibility for a 2-year public company.
- Makeup turnaround executed exactly as narrated: −1.3% (FY24) → +13.7% (FY25). Proves the portfolio model self-heals.
- Margin expansion (20.2%→20.7% EBITDA) + deleveraging to 0.7x *while* growing — clean compounding.
- Niche (Byredo) + APAC as durable new growth legs; Charlotte Tilbury *"distributed only in about 1/5 of the footprint it could be in"* (real runway, not hype).
- TTM FCF €1.28B, 64% conversion — cash generation is genuine.

🔴 **Red:**
- **The load-bearing catalyst — the Estée Lauder combination — was TERMINATED on 2026-05-21** (confirmed live; see §8). The single biggest re-rate driver in the file is *spent*, and the stock fell ~14% on the break. Any thesis built on deal optionality is dead as of three weeks before this writing.
- **CEO transition mid-stream:** founder Marc Puig → Chairman; Jose Manuel Muniesa CEO, one call of record, during the most consequential corporate event in the company's public life.
- **Growth decelerating sharply:** FY24 +10.9% → FY25 +7.8% → Q1-26 +4.7% LFL. The trend line is down, and reported growth is worse still after −4% FX.
- **Statutory gross margin flagged *contracting*** (70.4%, −0.35σ vs peak) under tariff + FX + mix pressure, even as management trumpets "record" adjusted margin.
- **ROIC 7.0%** vs ROE 28.6% — acquisition goodwill (Byredo, Tilbury, Sturm) means capital efficiency is mediocre; the bull "quality compounder" framing rests on ROE, not ROIC.
- **Charlotte Tilbury change-of-control clause** surfaced (in deal reporting) as a structural complication to *any* sale of Puig — a permanent overhang on the M&A-premium thesis.
- **Scan valuation is corrupt** (332% MoS / €64 fair value) — must not be used to size the position.

---

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Vocabulary shift Q1-26:** "market moderation," "more moderate consumer environment," "normalized from previously elevated levels," "softer trend in prestige" — this is the language of a *category coming off a post-COVID fragrance boom*, not of a structural grower. Management euphemizes it, but the fragrance super-cycle is visibly cooling, and Puig is most exposed to it (74% of Q1-26 sales).
- **The postponed Capital Markets Day** (April 14 → indefinite) is the clearest non-verbal signal in the file: you do not pull your first investor-day-as-public-company unless something binding is in motion.
- **"We will stay active on M&A" (Marc Puig, FY25)** reads very differently in hindsight — within ~5 weeks of that call the company was confirmed in talks to be *acquired by* Estée Lauder. The acquirer-framing was, at minimum, incomplete.
- **Charlotte Tilbury ownership mechanics:** Puig "will progressively assume full ownership until the beginning of 2031" (FY24 call) — and Tilbury's residual stake + change-of-control rights became a named deal-breaker. The crown-jewel brand carries embedded minority-holder friction.
- **New CFO too:** Q1-26 introduces Miquel Angel Serra as CFO (Joan Ramis/Albiol had been CFO across prior calls). A *simultaneous* CEO and CFO change is a fuller management reset than a routine succession — worth confirming the bench is intact.
- **APAC growth quality caveat:** management volunteered that FY25 APAC was lifted by subsidiary consolidation "we do not expect to repeat" — a subtle admission that the headline +21.7% overstates organic APAC momentum.

---

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: STABLE (operationally STRENGTHENING) business with a PIVOTING / RESET corporate-event situation.**

Puig is, on the evidence, a genuinely high-quality, well-managed prestige-beauty compounder with elite guidance credibility, real margin expansion, a clean balance sheet (~0.7x), and strong FCF (€1.28B TTM). The operating trajectory is *strengthening* (makeup fixed, niche/APAC added). If this were purely an operating-quality question, it would screen as a buy-the-quality name.

But the file's defining fact is a corporate event, and **the director must weigh one thing above all else:**

> **The load-bearing catalyst is dead.** The Estée Lauder business combination — the only event in this file capable of a step-change re-rate — was **terminated on 2026-05-21**, ~2.5 weeks before this review, on a valuation gap, controlling-family disagreement, and the Charlotte Tilbury change-of-control clause. The stock fell ~14% on the break and trades ~€16.3 today, *below* its 50-day average — i.e., the market is still digesting the disappointment, and the price already reflects the *absence* of a takeout. There is no live deal premium to capture; what remains is a standalone, decelerating (LFL +10.9%→+7.8%→+4.7%) compounder under a brand-new CEO/CFO, priced at ~11x P/FCF — reasonable but not a screaming bargain once the corrupt 332%-MoS model is discarded.

Before sizing, the director must decide whether they are underwriting **(a)** a *standalone quality compounder* at a fair-to-modestly-cheap multiple with a cooling fragrance cycle and an unproven new management team (a defensible 2–3% position on quality), or **(b)** a *renewed M&A target* betting Estée Lauder or another suitor returns — a thesis that just failed once on structural (not price-only) obstacles (Tilbury clause, dual-family control) and should be treated as SOFT optionality, not a hard catalyst. The scan's catalyst score of 0.5 and the dead deal both argue against (b). **Do not size this on the takeout; size it, if at all, on the standalone franchise — and haircut for decelerating growth, the management reset, and a fragrance super-cycle that management itself now calls "moderated."**

---
CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 4 | TRAJECTORY: STABLE
